quiz.title: Unit 2 Quiz 5, Grade 10 Biology
quiz.subtitle: Ecology: Energy Transfer & Ecological Efficiency
quiz.subject: Biology
quiz.topic: 10% Rule, Energy Loss, Chemosynthesis, Trophic Efficiency

questions.question: If producers capture 10,000 kJ of energy, how much energy is available to primary consumers?
questions.explanation: According to the 10% rule, only about 10% of energy transfers from one trophic level to the next. 10,000 kJ × 0.10 = 1,000 kJ available to primary consumers.

questions.question: Most energy lost between trophic levels is released as:
questions.explanation: Energy lost between trophic levels is primarily released as heat through metabolic processes like respiration. Unlike nutrients, energy cannot be recycled.

questions.question: Which trophic level generally has the greatest biomass?
questions.explanation: Producers typically have the greatest biomass in an ecosystem because they capture energy directly from the sun. Biomass decreases at each successive trophic level due to energy loss.

questions.question: Which type of organism produces energy in ecosystems without sunlight?
questions.explanation: Chemoautotrophs use chemical energy from inorganic compounds (like hydrogen sulfide) to produce food, forming the base of ecosystems without sunlight, such as deep-sea hydrothermal vents.

questions.question: Select all that apply. Which of the following can be found in South America?
questions.explanation: Anacondas and jaguars are native to South America. Pythons are native to Africa, Asia, and Australia; giraffes and zebras are native to Africa. None of these are naturally found in South America.

questions.question: Deep-sea hydrothermal vent ecosystems rely primarily on:
questions.explanation: Hydrothermal vent ecosystems lack sunlight, so producers (chemosynthetic bacteria) use chemosynthesis to convert chemicals from vent fluids into energy.

questions.question: Which statement best explains why most food chains rarely exceed four or five trophic levels?
options: Top predators require too much territory to hunt effectively beyond this level
Insufficient energy remains at higher levels to support a viable population
Competition becomes too intense for species to coexist at higher levels
Genetic mutations accumulate too rapidly in long food chain species
questions.explanation: Due to the 10% rule, energy availability decreases exponentially at each level. By the fourth or fifth level, there is rarely enough energy remaining to sustain a stable population of predators.

questions.question: How does consuming a plant-based diet compare to a meat-based diet in terms of land use efficiency?
options: Plant-based diets require more land because crops grow slower than livestock
Plant-based diets are more efficient because humans access energy at a lower trophic level
Meat-based diets are more efficient because animals concentrate energy from many plants
Both diets require similar land area because energy loss occurs only during digestion
questions.explanation: Eating plants places humans at the primary consumer level, accessing ~10% of producer energy. Eating meat places humans at secondary+ levels, accessing only ~1% or less, requiring more land to produce the same caloric output.

questions.question: What is the primary role of decomposers in the context of energy flow?
options: They recycle energy back to producers for reuse in photosynthesis
They extract remaining energy from dead matter and release it as heat
They convert solar energy into chemical energy for the detrital food web
They store energy in soil organic matter for future trophic levels
questions.explanation: Decomposers break down dead organic matter, extracting the last usable energy through respiration (released as heat). They cycle nutrients, but energy itself flows one-way and is not recycled.

questions.question: Why do ectothermic animals (e.g., fish, reptiles) generally demonstrate higher energy transfer efficiency than endothermic animals (e.g., mammals, birds)?
options: Ectotherms consume more food per unit of body mass than endotherms
Ectotherms do not expend energy maintaining a constant body temperature
Endotherms have slower metabolic rates that waste more energy over time
Endotherms digest food less efficiently due to higher internal body heat
questions.explanation: Endotherms use a significant portion of consumed energy for thermoregulation (metabolic heat). Ectotherms rely on external heat sources, allowing more consumed energy to be converted into biomass.

questions.question: In the wild, a lion and a tiger may never meet because ___________.
options: Lions live in African savannas, while tigers live in Asian forests
Lions live in African forests, while tigers live in Asian savannas
Lions live in African savannas, while tigers live in Asian savannas
Lions move in groups, while tigers roam solitarily
questions.explanation: Lions are native to African savannas and grasslands, while tigers are native to Asian forests and jungles. Their geographic ranges do not overlap in the wild, so they never naturally encounter each other despite both being big cats.

questions.question: The natural place of an organism or community is known as:
questions.explanation: Habitat is the natural environment where an organism or community lives. A niche refers to an organism's role, biome is a large geographic region, and habit refers to behavioral patterns.

questions.question: Which is the renewable exhaustible natural energy resource?
questions.explanation: Biomass is renewable (can be regrown) but exhaustible if overused. Coal, petroleum, and kerosene are non-renewable fossil fuels. Note: This question tests understanding that renewable ≠ infinite.

questions.question: Which of the following possesses the maximum energy?
questions.explanation: Primary producers (autotrophs) possess the maximum energy in an ecosystem because they capture energy directly from the sun through photosynthesis. According to the 10% rule, energy decreases at each successive trophic level, with primary consumers having less energy than producers, secondary consumers having even less, and decomposers obtaining energy from dead matter at various levels.

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You understand energy transfer efficiency and ecological pyramids.
scoreMessages.70: Good job. Review the 10% rule and differences between ectotherms and endotherms.
scoreMessages.50: Satisfactory. Focus on energy loss between trophic levels and chemosynthesis.
scoreMessages.0: Please review energy flow, trophic levels, and ecological efficiency concepts.
